This was also the period during which the Rigveda was composed by people living... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. full_notes:
Kings Farmers and Towns is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY 28 THEME TWO There were several developments in different parts of the subcontinent during the long span of 1,500 years following the end of the Harappan civilisation.

The text explains that this was also the period during which the Rigveda was composed by people living along the Indus and its tributaries.

The text explains that agricultural settlements emerged in many parts of the subcontinent, including north India, the Deccan Plateau, and parts of Karnataka.

The text explains that besides, there is evidence of pastoral populations in the Deccan and further south.

The text explains that new modes of disposal of the dead, including the making of elaborate stone structures known as megaliths, emerged in central and south India from the first millennium BCE.

The text explains that in many cases, the dead were buried with a rich range of iron tools and weapons.

The text explains that from c . sixth century B C E , there is evidence that there were other trends as well.

The text explains that perhaps the most visible was the emergence of early states, empires and kingdoms.

The text explains that underlying these political processes were other changes, evident in the ways in which agricultural production was organised.

The text explains that simultaneously, new towns appeared almost throughout the subcontinent.

The text explains that historians attempt to understand these developments by drawing on a range of sources – inscriptions, texts, coins and visual material.

The text explains that you will also notice that these sources do not tell the entire story.

The text explains that kings, Farmers and Towns Ear Ear Ear Ear Earl l l l ly S y S y S y S y St t t t tat at at at ates and Economies es and Economies es and Economies es and Economies es and Economies ((((( c. c. c. c. c.

The text explains that 600 600 600 600 600 BCE BCE BCE BCE BCE- 600 600 600 600 600 CE CE CE CE CE))))) Epigraphy is the study of inscriptions.

The text explains that prinsep and Piyadassi Some of the most momentous developments in Indian epigraphy took place in the 1830s.

The text explains that this was when James Prinsep, an officer in the mint of the East India Company, deciphered Brahmi and Kharosthi, two scripts used in the earliest inscriptions and coins.

The text explains that this gave a new direction to investigations into early Indian political history as European and Indian scholars used inscriptions and texts composed in a variety of languages to reconstruct the lineages of major dynasties that had ruled the subcontinent.

The text explains that as a result, the broad contours of political history were in place by the early decades of the twentieth century.

The text explains that subsequently, scholars began to shift their focus to the context of political history, investigating whether there were connections between political changes and economic and social developments.

The text explains that it was soon realised that while there were links, these were not always simple or direct.

The text explains that the Earliest States 2.1 The sixteen mahajanapadas The sixth century BCE is often regarded as a major turning point in early Indian history.

The text explains that it also witnessed the growth of diverse systems of thought, including Buddhism and Jainism.

The text explains that early Buddhist and Jaina texts (see also Chapter 4) mention, amongst other things, sixteen states known as mahajanapadas.

The text explains that both Mahavira and the Buddha (Chapter 4) belonged to such ganas.

The text explains that in some instances, as in the case of the Vajji sangha, the rajas probably controlled resources such as land collectively.

The text explains that although their histories are often difficult to reconstruct due to the lack of sources, some of these states lasted for nearly a thousand years.

The text explains that maintaining these fortified cities as well as providing for incipient armies and bureaucracies required resources.

The text explains that names of rulers such as Ajatasattu and Asoka, known from Prakrit texts and inscriptions, have been spelt in their Prakrit forms in this chapter.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY 30 Map 1 Early states and their capitals Arabian Sea Bay of Bengal KAMBOJA GANDHARA Pushkalavati Taxila SHURASENA Mathura MATSYA Indraprastha Ahichchhatra KURUPANCHALA Kaushambi CHEDI AVANTI Ujjayini ASHMAKA VATSA Shravasti VANGA Champa ANGA Rajgir MAGADHA Vaishali VAJJI (VRIJJI) Kusinagara MALLA Varanasi KASHI KOSHALA Sketch map not to scale century BCE onwards, Brahmanas began composing Sanskrit texts known as the Dharmasutras.

The text explains that these laid down norms for rulers (as well as for other social categories), who were ideally expected to be Kshatriyas (see also Chapter 3).

The text explains that were resources also procured from pastoralists and forest peoples?

The text explains that oligarchy refers to a form of government where power is exercised by a group of men.

The text explains that modern historians explain this development in a variety of ways: Magadha was a region where agriculture was especially productive.

The text explains that besides, iron mines (in present-day Jharkhand) were accessible and provided resources for tools and weapons.

The text explains that 3.1 Finding out about the Mauryas Historians have used a variety of sources to reconstruct the history of the Mauryan Empire.

The text explains that another source that is often used is the Arthashastra, parts of which were probably composed by Kautilya or Chanakya, traditionally believed to be the minister of Chandragupta.

The text explains that 272/268-231 BCE) on rocks and pillars are often regarded as amongst the most valuable sources.

The text explains that asoka was the first ruler who inscribed his messages to his subjects and officials on stone surfaces – natural rocks as well as polished pillars.

The text explains that kINGS, FARMERS AND TOWNS Arabian Sea Bay of Bengal Mansehra Shahbazgarhi Kandahar Taxila Kalsi Topra Meerut Bahapur Bairat Bhabru Girnar Sopara Ujjayini Sanchi Nigalisagar Rummindei Rampurwa Lauriya Nandangarh Lauriya Araraj Gujarra Kaushambi Ahraura Sarnath Sahasram Pataliputra Shishupalgarh Jaugada KALINGA Sannati Maski Gavimath Palkigundu Jatinga Rameshwar Siddapur Brahmagiri Nittur Rajula Mandagiri Udegolam CHOLAS PANDYAS KERALAPUTRAS Sketch map not to scale p MAJOR ROCK EDICTS MINOR ROCK EDICTS PILLAR INSCRIPTIONS Map 2 Distribution of Asokan inscriptions Ü Could rulers have engraved inscriptions in areas that were not included within their empire?

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY 34 Communication along both land and riverine routes was vital for the existence of the empire.

The text explains that when historians began reconstructing early Indian history in the nineteenth century, the emergence of the Mauryan Empire was regarded as a major landmark.

The text explains that read the excerpts from Megasthenes and the Arthashastra (Sources 1 and 2).

The text explains that to what extent do you think these texts are useful in reconstructing a history of Mauryan administration?

The text explains that it lasted for about 150 years, which is not a very long time in the vast span of the history of the subcontinent.

The text explains that we know about these states from a variety of sources.

The text explains that for instance, the early Tamil Sangam texts (see also Chapter 3) contain poems describing chiefs and the ways in which they acquired and distributed resources.

The text explains that their social origins were often obscure, but, as we will see in the case of the Satavahanas (Chapter 3), once they acquired power they attempted to claim social status in a variety of ways.

The text explains that are any Asokan inscriptions found in these areas?

The text explains that cut and paste all the statements made by government officials about public works.

The text explains that note what the reports say about the resources required for such projects, how the resources are mobilised and the objective of the project.

The text explains that who issues these statements, and how and why are they communicated?

The text explains that compare and contrast these with the evidence from inscriptions discussed in this chapter.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History I, Chapter 2: Kings Farmers and Towns

schemes.point: Capturing elephants for the army The Arthashastra lays down minute details of administrative and military organisation. (NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History I Ch2 pg 7)

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Kings Farmers and Towns, which statement is supported by the source text about THEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY 30?
options: It is an era associated with early states, cities, the growing use of iron, the development of coinage, etc.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Kings Farmers and Towns, which statement is supported by the source text about KAMBOJA?
options: Clearly, these were amongst the most important mahajanapadas.
While most mahajanapadas were ruled by kings, some, known as ganas or sanghas, were oligarchies (p.
30), where power was shared by a number of men, often collectively called rajas.
Both Mahavira and the Buddha (Chapter 4) belonged to such ganas.

options: From c. sixth Inscriptions Inscriptions are writings engraved on hard surfaces such as stone, metal or pottery.
They usually record the achievements, activities or ideas of those who commissioned them and include the exploits of kings, or donations made by women...
Inscriptions are virtually permanent records, some of which carry dates.
Others are dated on the basis of palaeography or styles of writing, with a fair amount of precision.
